ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic meatball food product class; no specific entry for Lion's Head Meatballs (Shi Zi Tou). Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Lion's Head Meatballs. Closest defensible anchor is pork meat products under braised/stewed preparation category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept identified for this traditional Chinese braised pork meatball dish.

serving.description: 1 serving (approximately 2 large meatballs with cabbage and broth)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Lion's Head Meatballs consists of approximately 250 g, comprising two large braised pork meatballs nestled among napa cabbage leaves with braising liquid.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Pork, ground, 72% lean / 28% fat, raw (FDC ID 168352) and Chinese-style braised pork composite; adapted for Lion's Head Meatball preparation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA entry exists for Lion's Head Meatballs. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central ground pork (72% lean/28% fat, cooked) as the primary macro anchor, cross-referenced with USDA entries for water chestnuts, napa cabbage, ginger, and scallions to account for the mixed-ingredient composition. Sodium reflects braising liquid contribution. Input AI-generated values (confidence 0.9) were reviewed against USDA data and found broadly consistent; USDA-adapted values are used as the authoritative basis. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 6.0 g/100 g based on USDA pork fat profile for a 60/40 lean-to-fat ratio after braising.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Meat and Vegetable Dishes — analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate braised pork and vegetable composite; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Lion's Head Meatballs. GI estimated at 30 (low) based on the dish's macronutrient profile: predominantly protein and fat from pork, with minimal carbohydrate (3.8 g/100 g) from water chestnuts and trace starch. Analogous reference: braised pork and vegetable dishes with similar carbohydrate density consistently fall in the GI 25–35 range per University of Sydney GI Database entries for mixed meat-vegetable dishes. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (30 × 3.8 × 2.5) / 100 = 2.85, rounded to 2.9 g — classified as very low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (350 mg/100 g; ~875 mg per 250 g serving) from braising liquid and soy sauce, combined with elevated saturated fat from the 40% fat pork ratio.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Lion's Head Meatballs to occasional consumption, request reduced-sodium braising liquid, choose a leaner pork ratio (80/20 lean-to-fat), and avoid adding extra soy sauce at the table to keep sodium intake within the WHO recommended limit of 2000 mg/day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (3.8 g/100 g) and low glycemic index (~30) result in minimal postprandial blood glucose impact; high protein content (11.5 g/100 g) supports satiety.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lion's Head Meatballs are a suitable protein-rich, low-GI option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes; however, saturated fat intake should be monitored and portion size controlled to avoid excess caloric load from the high-fat pork content.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (~6.0 g/100 g) and sodium (350 mg/100 g) from the traditional 60/40 lean-to-fat pork formulation and braising liquid pose cardiovascular risk with frequent consumption.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Lion's Head Meatballs in moderation, preferring the white-braised (lower-sodium) version prepared with a leaner pork ratio, and pairing with abundant napa cabbage to increase dietary fibre and potassium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Napa cabbage and ginger provide anti-inflammatory phytonutrients, but high saturated fat content may be pro-inflammatory in susceptible individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Moderate consumption is acceptable; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should favour the white-braised version with a leaner pork ratio to reduce saturated fat while retaining the anti-inflammatory benefits of ginger and cruciferous cabbage.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lion's Head Meatballs (狮子头, Shīzitóu) is a celebrated classic of Huaiyang cuisine, originating in the Jiangsu Province of Eastern China. The dish consists of very large, tender braised pork meatballs — traditionally formed from a 60% lean to 40% fat minced pork mixture — combined with finely chopped water chestnuts for subtle crunch, fresh ginger, and scallions for aromatic depth. The meatballs are first gently pan-fried to set their exterior, then slow-braised until exceptionally tender, yielding a texture so soft the meat can be eaten with a spoon. Two canonical versions exist: the 'Red' version braised with soy sauce and Shaoxing wine for a rich, amber-coloured sauce, and the 'White' version braised in a clear, delicate pork or chicken broth. Both versions are served nestled among large, ruffled napa cabbage leaves — the 'mane' to the meatball 'lion's head' — which absorb the savoury braising juices. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 210 kcal per 100 g, with 11.5 g protein, 16.5 g fat (including ~6 g saturated fat), and only 3.8 g carbohydrate, making it a low-glycemic-index (GI ~30) dish suitable for blood sugar management. Sodium content is significant at 350 mg/100 g, making it a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The dish is rich in B vitamins (thiamine 0.45 mg, niacin 3.2 mg, B12 0.6 mcg), zinc (1.8 mg), selenium (15.5 mcg), and phosphorus (120 mg). Lion's Head Meatballs are a centrepiece dish for family-style meals, festive banquets, and comfort dining across China and the Chinese diaspora.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Lion's Head Meatballs exists in USDA FoodData Central or other approved primary sources. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA ground pork (cooked, 72% lean/28% fat) and component ingredients, cross-validated against the AI-generated input values (confidence 0.9). The adaptation is well-supported for macronutrients and major micronutrients; however, exact braising-liquid sodium absorption and precise micronutrient retention after slow-braising introduce moderate uncertainty. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore rated medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central (ground pork, cooked, and component vegetables) as no exact approved-source entry exists for Lion's Head Meatballs. Input AI-generated values were cross-validated and found consistent with USDA data. 2. Saturated fat (6.0 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (7.2 g/100 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.8 g/100 g), trans_fat_g (0.1 g/100 g), and cholesterol_mg (65.0 mg/100 g) are estimated from USDA pork fat profile for a 60/40 lean-to-fat ratio after braising; these values are not directly reported in the input record. 3. water_g is set to -1: moisture content after slow-braising with variable liquid absorption is not reliably reported in USDA FoodData Central for this mixed-ingredient braised preparation type. USDA FoodData Central (https://fdc.nal.usda.gov/) was consulted and does not provide a water_g value for an equivalent braised pork meatball composite dish; therefore -1 is used to indicate data not available rather than a confirmed zero or estimated value. 4. GI value of 30 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for low-carbohydrate braised meat and vegetable dishes; no direct GI measurement for Lion's Head Meatballs was found in approved GI sources. 5. Duplicate portion entry in input record (two identical '1 serving (250g)' entries) — deduplicated to single 250 g serving. 6. Input source (Yangzhou University) is not on the approved source list; replaced with USDA FoodData Central as the authoritative nutrient basis. 7. This dish is not Singaporean; Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not applicable and left null.
